planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring with another rabbiteye.

directions: Brightwell is a heavy-bearing rabbiteye that pairs well with Tifblue and Powderblue for pollination. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and it will tolerate heat and short dry spells once established. The medium, sweet berries ripen midseason on an upright, vigorous bush.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring with a compatible pollinizer.

directions: Austin is an early rabbiteye that produces large berries with a mild, sweet flavor. Plant it near another early-blooming rabbiteye and give it acidic, well-drained soil. The fruit ripens early in the rabbiteye season and drops readily when fully ripe, so pick promptly.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring with another rabbiteye.

directions: Woodard is an older, spreading rabbiteye that yields large, tangy berries early in the season. Plant with a compatible pollinizer and provide acidic, well-drained soil. Its lower, wider habit makes it easy to harvest, though the ripe fruit is best picked quickly before it softens.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring with another late rabbiteye.

directions: Ochlockonee is a very late-ripening rabbiteye that extends the harvest into late summer. Plant with Tifblue or Powderblue for pollination and give it acidic, well-drained soil. The firm, light-blue berries have a good flavor and store well after picking.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring; blooms late to avoid frost.

directions: Alapaha is an early-fruiting rabbiteye with a late bloom that helps it dodge spring frosts. Plant with another early rabbiteye for pollination and provide acidic, well-drained soil. The small to medium berries have a firm texture and sweet flavor, ripening early in the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring; ideal for containers and small spaces.

directions: Top Hat is a dwarf, self-fertile blueberry bred for containers, edging, and tiny gardens. Grow it in acidic potting mix or amended soil, keep it evenly moist, and enjoy the neat mound of white spring flowers and fall color. Despite its small size it yields a surprising crop of full-flavored berries in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; bred for cold-climate gardens.

directions: Northblue is a half-high hybrid combining highbush fruit quality with lowbush cold hardiness. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch well, as the shorter stature lets snow cover protect it through hard winters. It bears large, dark, flavorful berries in midsummer on a compact bush.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; extremely cold-hardy and low-growing.

directions: Northsky is one of the shortest half-high hybrids, hugging the ground where snow cover shields it from extreme cold. Plant in acidic soil and mulch to keep the shallow roots moist and cool. The sky-blue berries have a sweet, wild-blueberry flavor and ripen in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in cold-winter regions.

directions: Northcountry is a hardy half-high hybrid with a sweet, mild berry reminiscent of wild lowbush fruit. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch to protect the roots through cold winters. Its low, rounded form and good fall color make it attractive as well as productive, with berries ripening in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; developed for northern gardens.

directions: Chippewa is a cold-hardy half-high hybrid that produces sweet, sky-blue berries on a compact bush. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and mulch heavily to insulate the roots. It is partly self-fertile but crops best with another half-high or highbush nearby, ripening in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in early spring; pair with another half-high for pollination.

directions: Polaris is an early-ripening half-high hybrid with an aromatic, sweet berry and excellent cold hardiness. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil and add a compatible pollinizer such as Chippewa for the best fruit set. The firm berries ripen early in summer on a compact, tidy bush.

planting.note: Set dormant crowns in early spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant crowns so the midpoint sits level with the soil surface, keeping the growing tip exposed. This early Canadian variety ripens intensely sweet berries and shrugs off cold winters. Pinch the first-year flowers to build vigorous plants, then pick fully red fruit every couple of days at peak season.

planting.note: Set crowns in spring as soon as the soil thaws and drains.

directions: Space crowns in rows and firm the soil around the roots without burying the crown. This very early Nova Scotia selection delivers the first crop of the season with glossy, medium-large fruit. Mulch with straw to keep berries clean and harvest when the shoulders color fully.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in fertile, well-drained ground.

directions: Set crowns at the correct depth and water in thoroughly after planting. This USDA-Cornell midseason release resists red stele root rot and yields firm, richly flavored berries. Renovate the bed after fruiting by mowing old foliage and thinning crowded runners.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring once frost danger eases and soil is workable.

directions: Position each crown so roots fan downward and the bud sits just at soil level. This USDA-bred midseason variety is prized for exceptional dessert flavor and disease resistance. Keep beds evenly moist during fruit swell and gather berries when uniformly deep red.

planting.note: Plant crowns around the last spring frost for a long fruiting season.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil and water in well. This French everbearer produces aromatic, heart-shaped berries with a wild-strawberry sweetness from early summer to fall. Feed lightly through the season and pick continuously to keep new flowers forming.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in rich, free-draining soil.

directions: Firm each crown into loosened soil with the growing point uncovered. This classic French heirloom bears elongated, tangy-sweet berries treasured by chefs. The soft fruit bruises easily, so harvest gently the moment it colors and eat or use it fresh.

planting.note: Set crowns in spring in fertile ground with good drainage.

directions: Plant crowns with roots spread out and the crown resting at the surface. This widely grown commercial midseason variety produces large, glossy, well-flavored berries with excellent shelf life. Protect the bed from botrytis with airflow and pick fruit dry whenever possible.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in a sunny, sheltered bed.

directions: Set crowns at soil level and water in to settle the roots. This Dutch Elsanta seedling offers uniform, sweet berries and tolerates warm weather better than its parent. Mulch beneath the plants to keep fruit clean and harvest at full color for best sugar.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring for a very late-season crop.

directions: Give this vigorous variety a little extra spacing and set crowns at the correct depth. Malwina is one of the latest-ripening strawberries, extending the fresh harvest with dark, richly aromatic berries. Provide steady moisture during its extended fruiting and pick when deeply colored.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring in well-drained, fertile soil.

directions: Set crowns level and firm the soil to eliminate air pockets around the roots. This late British variety combines strong disease resistance with large, firm, well-flavored fruit. Water consistently as berries swell and harvest each one when it reaches full deep red.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in an open, sunny position.

directions: Spread the roots in the planting hole and set the crown at soil level. This reliable British heirloom is dependable and forgiving, producing consistent midseason crops of medium berries. Tuck straw under the trusses to protect ripening fruit and pick when evenly red.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring in rich soil with generous compost.

directions: Enrich the bed before planting and set crowns with the bud just above soil. This old British favorite is famed for outstanding flavor though it yields modestly and needs good care. Water and feed steadily, and harvest the fragrant early berries as soon as they ripen.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring in loose, fertile ground.

directions: Set crowns carefully at soil level and keep newly planted beds moist. This historic American heirloom, once called the finest-flavored strawberry, produces large dark-red dessert berries. It has little disease resistance, so give it clean soil and airy spacing, and pick fully ripe.

planting.note: Plant crowns near the last frost for summer-long fruiting.

directions: Set crowns at soil level and provide steady water through the warm months. This day-neutral variety yields large, very sweet berries continuously and adapts well to containers. Feed regularly during the long harvest and pick fruit as soon as it fully colors.

planting.note: In warm climates plant crowns in fall for a winter-to-spring crop.

directions: Set crowns into raised, mulched beds with the growing point exposed. This University of Florida short-day variety fruits early in mild-winter regions with firm, bright berries. Keep soil evenly moist and harvest frequently once fruit reaches full color.

planting.note: Plant crowns in fall in mild regions for winter-spring harvest.

directions: Plant crowns into well-drained, mulched beds and water in gently. This University of Florida release (Florida127) produces large, aromatic, sweet berries with strong flavor. Maintain consistent moisture and pick each berry as soon as it turns fully red.

planting.note: Plant crowns in fall in mild climates or early spring elsewhere.

directions: Set crowns at soil level in fertile, well-drained beds. This University of California short-day variety yields firm, dark-red berries on compact plants suited to warm regions. Water consistently, watch for foliar disease, and harvest fruit at full color.

planting.note: Plant crowns in fall in mild-winter areas for an early crop.

directions: Plant crowns into raised beds with the bud kept above the soil surface. This University of California variety produces large, firm, glossy berries early in the season. Provide even moisture and harvest promptly as fruit ripens to keep plants productive.

planting.note: Plant crowns near the last frost for continuous summer fruit.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil and keep beds evenly watered in heat. This University of California day-neutral variety fruits through summer with firm, flavorful berries and good disease tolerance. Feed lightly during cropping and pick fruit as it fully colors.

planting.note: Plant crowns near the last frost for a long day-neutral season.

directions: Plant crowns at soil level in fertile, well-drained ground. This University of California day-neutral selection produces firm, light-colored, mildly sweet berries over a long window. Maintain steady moisture and harvest regularly to encourage ongoing bloom.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in well-drained, fertile soil.

directions: Set crowns at the proper depth and water in to settle the roots. This USDA midseason variety offers broad disease resistance and firm, flavorful berries with good adaptability. Renovate the matted row after harvest and pick fruit at full deep-red ripeness.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring; very cold-hardy for northern beds.

directions: Set crowns level and firm the soil around the roots. This University of Minnesota variety is exceptionally winter-hardy and yields sweet, aromatic berries with strong disease resistance. Mulch heavily for winter in cold zones and harvest at full color.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Spread roots and set crowns at soil level, watering in well. This Cornell early-midseason variety bears large, glossy, heart-shaped berries with excellent aroma and flavor. Keep beds weed-free, refresh mulch, and harvest fruit at full ripeness.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: Set crowns level with the surface and firm the soil gently. This popular Italian early variety produces uniform, sweet, glossy berries that resist rain damage well. Give it a sheltered site, keep soil evenly moist, and pick fruit at full color.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in fertile ground.

directions: Set crowns at soil level and water thoroughly after planting. This Dutch midseason variety yields soft, juicy, deeply flavored dark-red berries ideal for fresh eating and jam. The fruit is tender, so harvest gently and use it soon after picking.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring in rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant crowns level with the soil and keep beds evenly moist. This Dutch mid-to-late variety produces dark, glossy, intensely sweet berries excellent for preserves. Mulch to keep fruit clean and pick at full deep-red ripeness for the best flavor.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; tolerates lighter, drier soils well.

directions: Set crowns at soil level and firm around the roots. This Belgian midseason variety crops heavily with large, sweet berries and performs well even on sandy ground. Water during dry spells as fruit forms and harvest when the berries color fully.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in well-drained soil.

directions: Set crowns level with the surface and water in well. This early Canadian variety produces small-to-medium bright-red berries with excellent sweet flavor for fresh use. The soft fruit does not ship well, so pick it ripe and enjoy it quickly.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring; hardy for northern gardens.

directions: Set crowns at the correct depth and water in to firm the soil. This hardy Nova Scotia variety produces good yields of bright, flavorful berries and adapts to cold climates. Mulch for winter protection and harvest fruit at full red color.

planting.note: Plant crowns near the last frost for a long fruiting season.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil and keep beds evenly moist. This everbearing variety continues fruiting in summer heat that stops many others, producing sweet, firm berries. Feed lightly through the season and pick continuously to sustain new bloom.

planting.note: Start seed indoors well before the last frost and transplant after it passes.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on moist mix, press in lightly without covering, and keep warm and bright. This F1 everbearer flowers in its first year from seed and yields sweet berries all summer, ideal for pots and baskets. Harden off, transplant after frost, and pick fruit as it colors.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and set out plants after frost.

directions: Press the tiny seeds onto moist mix and leave uncovered under bright light. This runnerless alpine bears small creamy-white berries that birds tend to ignore, with intense sweet pineapple-like flavor. Transplant after frost, keep evenly moist, and pick soft, aromatic fruit through the season.

planting.note: Plant divisions or runners in spring in a sunny to partly shaded bed.

directions: Set plants at soil level and let them spread by runners as a groundcover. This strawberry-cinquefoil hybrid produces showy rose-pink flowers all summer and occasional small edible berries. Water in dry spells and shear lightly if growth becomes untidy to keep blooms coming.

planting.note: Plant divisions or runners in spring as a flowering groundcover.

directions: Set plants level with the soil and allow runners to knit into a mat. This ornamental strawberry hybrid bears bright cherry-red flowers through summer with small edible berries following. Keep soil moist, divide crowded clumps in spring, and enjoy both the color and the occasional fruit.

planting.note: Plant crowns near the last frost for a late, extended crop.

directions: Set crowns level and keep beds consistently watered through summer. This British everbearer fruits from midsummer into autumn with large, glossy, sweet-sharp berries. Feed during cropping and pick continuously to keep the plants flowering late into the season.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Set crowns level with the surface and firm the soil gently. This modern British early-midseason variety yields heavy crops of large, sweet, glossy berries with excellent flavor. Keep beds weed-free and evenly moist, and harvest fruit at full red color.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter, chill briefly, then transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed, keep it lightly moist, and give it a cool spell to improve germination. This Eurasian wild species produces small greenish-white berries that flush pink-red and carry a sweet, musky flavor. Grow it in lean, well-drained soil, water sparingly, and pick when the fruit softens and detaches easily.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in fertile, free-draining soil.

directions: Set crowns level and water in to settle the roots. This early variety produces firm, deep-red, well-flavored berries on compact, reliable plants. Mulch beneath the fruit to keep it clean and pick each berry once it colors fully.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring; well suited to the southern United States.

directions: Set crowns at soil level and keep newly planted beds moist. This Arkansas variety produces large, firm, bright-red berries and shows good disease resistance in warm regions. Water steadily as fruit swells and harvest at full color for best flavor.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring; very hardy for cold climates.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil and firm the roots in place. This tough Canadian variety withstands harsh winters and bears medium, firm, bright-red berries reliably. Mulch for winter in cold zones, renovate after harvest, and pick fruit at full ripeness.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring for a very late-season crop.

directions: Set crowns level and water in well after planting. This late British variety extends the season with heavy yields of large, firm, sweet berries. Keep soil evenly moist through the extended fruiting window and harvest each berry at full deep-red color.

planting.note: Plant crowns near the last frost for a long cropping season.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil and maintain even moisture through summer. This British everbearer produces glossy, sweet, well-shaped berries over a long season on tidy plants. Feed lightly while cropping and pick fruit regularly to sustain flowering.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Set crowns level and firm the soil around the roots. This vigorous late British variety offers good disease resistance and heavy crops of large, firm, flavorful berries. Water through fruiting, keep the bed clean, and harvest berries at full color.

planting.note: Plant crowns in early spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Set crowns level with the surface and water in to settle them. This early British variety produces good yields of large, glossy, mildly sweet berries and crops well under cover. Keep soil evenly moist and pick fruit as soon as it colors fully.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring for a late-season harvest.

directions: Set crowns level and firm the soil to remove air pockets. This late Scottish-bred variety shows strong disease resistance and bears firm, bright, well-flavored berries. Provide steady moisture as fruit ripens and harvest at full deep-red color.

planting.note: Plant crowns in spring in fertile, well-drained ground.

directions: Set crowns level with the soil and water in thoroughly. This late Scottish variety resists mildew and wilt while producing glossy, firm, sweet berries with good yields. Keep beds evenly moist through the season and pick fruit at full ripeness.

planting.note: Set out bare-root or potted plants in early spring as soon as the ground can be worked.

directions: Plant in acidic soil around pH 4.5 to 5.2, keeping the crown level with the surrounding grade and mulching with pine bark. This compact early-season variety ripens ahead of most highbush types, so pick when berries turn fully blue and release with a gentle tug. Water steadily during fruit swell and prune out the oldest canes each winter to keep the bush productive.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring while the plant is still dormant.

directions: Dig a wide hole and blend in peat or composted pine to lower soil pH before setting the root ball. Collins bears firm, light-blue fruit of good dessert quality across the early to mid part of the season. Keep the root zone evenly moist and shallow-mulched, since the fibrous roots sit near the surface and dry out quickly.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in acidic, well-drained ground.

directions: Give this vigorous late-season variety room, as it grows tall and spreading with age. The large, aromatic berries hold well on the bush and develop a tangy-sweet flavor when left to color completely before picking. Feed with an acidifying fertilizer in spring and renew mulch yearly to protect the shallow roots.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring.

directions: Prized by home growers for exceptional flavor, Herbert produces large, dark-blue berries in the mid-to-late season. Plant in acidic soil with steady moisture and mulch heavily to keep roots cool. Harvest when the fruit fully colors and comes free easily, sampling a berry or two to confirm sweetness.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in acidic soil.

directions: This mid-season variety forms a compact, spreading bush with attractive golden winter stems. Berries are firm, light blue, and cluster tightly, making them easy to harvest in a single pick. Keep the soil acidic and evenly moist, and thin out crowded interior wood in late winter for better light and airflow.

planting.note: Transplant in early spring; tolerates a wider range of soils than most.

directions: A tough, adaptable New Zealand variety that establishes fast and yields heavily early in the season. The dark-blue berries have a rich, slightly wild flavor and ripen over an extended window. Plant in acidified soil, water consistently through summer, and remove weak twiggy growth each dormant season.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring.

directions: An upright, vigorous variety carrying large, sweet berries through the mid-season. Its part-southern parentage gives it good vigor and reliable cropping across a broad range. Keep the pH low with pine-bark mulch, water evenly during fruiting, and prune to keep an open, upright framework.

planting.note: Set out in early spring while dormant.

directions: One of the older reliable varieties, Rancocas is a productive, disease-tolerant bush bearing small-to-medium mid-season berries. It suckers freely and stays upright, so thin excess canes to keep it manageable. Grow in acidic, moist soil and harvest in several passes as the fruit colors unevenly.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in acidified ground.

directions: An heirloom variety valued for its aromatic, spicy-sweet mid-season fruit on an upright, moderately vigorous bush. Berries are medium-sized and best eaten fresh soon after picking. Keep soil pH low, mulch to conserve moisture, and prune older wood to maintain vigor and berry size.

planting.note: Transplant dormant plants in early spring.

directions: A late-season heirloom producing very large, richly flavored berries on a vigorous, spreading bush. The fruit can vary in size within a cluster, so harvest the largest ripe berries first. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil, water steadily during summer, and prune to keep the tall canes open.

planting.note: Plant in early spring.

directions: A large, vigorous, high-yielding bush that carries heavy crops of medium-sized dark-blue fruit in the mid-to-late season. Its strong growth suits it to open sites where it has room to spread. Grow in low-pH, moisture-retentive soil and thin out the oldest canes each winter to sustain production.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring.

directions: One of the earliest-ripening varieties, Weymouth yields medium, dark-blue berries with a soft texture best used fresh or for baking. The bush is compact and reliable, good for short-season gardens. Keep the acidic soil evenly moist and pick promptly, as the early fruit softens quickly on the bush.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in the warm South.

directions: This late-season rabbiteye ripens sweet, low-acid berries that turn slightly pink-red before finishing blue. Plant at least two rabbiteye varieties nearby for cross-pollination and a fuller crop. Grow in acidic, well-drained soil, water through the long ripening period, and expect the bush to reach large size over time.

planting.note: Best planted in fall or early spring in the South.

directions: Bluebelle bears large, firm, aromatic mid-season berries with a distinctive rich flavor. Pair it with another rabbiteye for pollination to maximize yield. Set plants in acidic soil, keep the root zone mulched and moist, and prune out crowded old canes to open up the tall, bushy frame.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring.

directions: A moderately compact rabbiteye producing firm, medium mid-season berries on a densely branched bush. Its tighter habit makes it easier to fit into smaller yards than most rabbiteyes. Grow in acidic soil with a second variety for pollination, and water consistently as the fruit fills out.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in the South.

directions: A classic late-season rabbiteye that yields abundant small-to-medium sweet berries and makes a handsome landscape shrub. Plant with a companion rabbiteye variety for good fruit set. Give it acidic, well-drained soil, steady summer moisture, and light annual pruning to keep it vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring.

directions: One of the latest rabbiteyes to ripen, Baldwin extends the harvest well into summer with firm, dark, medium-large berries. Its long ripening season makes it valuable for stretching out fresh picking. Grow in acidic soil with another rabbiteye for pollination, and keep the plant watered through the extended fruiting window.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring.

directions: An upright, vigorous late-season rabbiteye bearing firm, dark-blue berries in heavy clusters. The strong upright habit makes for easy harvesting and a tidy landscape form. Plant in acidic soil alongside another rabbiteye, water regularly, and remove the oldest canes periodically to renew the bush.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring; useful as a pollinizer.

directions: Montgomery is an early-flowering rabbiteye often planted to help pollinate other varieties, and it bears its own crop of medium mid-season berries. Site it near companion rabbiteyes so both benefit from the overlapping bloom. Grow in acidic, moist soil and prune lightly each winter to keep growth balanced.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring.

directions: A mid-to-late-season rabbiteye producing large, firm berries with good flavor and a small dry picking scar. Its vigorous upright growth carries heavy crops when paired with another rabbiteye. Plant in acidic soil, mulch well, and water steadily through the summer ripening period.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in the South.

directions: Titan is known for exceptionally large, quarter-sized berries with mild, sweet flavor on a tall, vigorous bush. It flowers late enough to often escape spring frost damage. Plant with another rabbiteye for pollination, grow in acidic soil, and keep it evenly watered so the big berries fill out fully.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring.

directions: An early-ripening rabbiteye that produces firm, medium-large berries over a long harvest window. Its early bloom pairs well with other early rabbiteyes for cross-pollination. Grow in acidic, well-drained soil, water regularly, and thin older canes as the bush matures to keep berry size up.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring; late bloom helps dodge frost.

directions: A late-blooming southern highbush that resists spring-frost injury while still fruiting in the mid-season. Berries are large, firm, and light blue with excellent flavor and shelf life. Plant in acidic soil, keep evenly moist, and prune to an open framework to sustain berry size on the vigorous bush.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in mild-winter areas.

directions: A low-chill southern highbush suited to warm climates, bearing firm, sweet berries with a small dry scar. Its upright habit and modest chill requirement make it a good choice for the Deep South. Grow in acidic, well-drained soil, water consistently, and prune to keep the interior open and productive.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in the South.

directions: A vigorous, high-yielding southern highbush producing firm, medium-large berries early in the season. It crops heavily and benefits from a nearby pollinizer for the best set. Plant in acidic soil, keep the shallow roots mulched and moist, and thin fruit-heavy canes to maintain berry size.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring.

directions: An upright, narrow southern highbush prized for crisp, firm berries with a distinctive crunchy texture. The stiff, erect canes make it easy to pick and space efficiently. Grow in acidic soil with steady moisture and remove crowded interior growth each winter to keep the plant vigorous.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in the South.

directions: A widely adapted southern highbush that fruits early with medium, firm berries and tolerates a range of soils. Its dependable cropping made it a longtime standard in the Southeast. Plant in acidified soil, keep evenly watered, and pair with another southern highbush for improved fruit set.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring.

directions: A mid-season southern highbush bearing firm, medium-large berries with a pleasant sweet-tart balance. It grows into a robust, spreading bush that crops reliably in warm regions. Grow in acidic, well-drained soil, water regularly during fruiting, and prune to keep an open, well-lit canopy.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in the South.

directions: A compact, spreading southern highbush that ripens early with firm, sweet, medium berries. Its smaller stature suits containers and tight garden spaces. Plant in acidic soil, keep the roots mulched and evenly moist, and lightly prune each winter to maintain shape and fruiting wood.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring.

directions: An upright, adaptable southern highbush bearing firm, sweet, medium berries in the mid-season. It tolerates heavier soils better than many highbush types and crops dependably. Grow in acidified, well-drained ground, water consistently, and prune older canes to renew productivity.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in the South.

directions: One of the earliest-ripening southern highbush varieties, Rebel yields firm, sweet berries well ahead of most types. Its early, heavy crop is a favorite for fresh markets and home gardens alike. Plant in acidic soil, water steadily through fruit set, and thin canes to keep the fast-growing bush productive.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring.

directions: A productive southern highbush bearing firm, flavorful berries on an upright, easily managed bush. Its consistent crops and good berry quality make it popular across the Southeast. Grow in acidic soil, keep evenly moist, and prune to maintain an open canopy for even ripening.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in mild areas.

directions: A low-chill southern highbush famous for its remarkably crisp, crunchy texture and sweet flavor. Best suited to warm-winter regions, it makes an excellent fresh-eating berry. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil, water consistently, and harvest when berries are fully blue and firm to the touch.

planting.note: Plant in fall or early spring in warm regions.

directions: An extremely low-chill, very early southern highbush that flowers and fruits well ahead of most varieties. It is well suited to the warmest blueberry regions where chill hours are scarce. Grow in acidic soil, protect the early bloom from late frosts, and water steadily as the fruit ripens.

planting.note: Establish from divisions or seedlings in spring in acidic, sandy soil.

directions: This low, spreading northern wild blueberry has soft, downy leaves and forms colonies by underground runners. It thrives in poor, sandy, highly acidic soils where little else fruits well. Plant in a sunny, lean site, water lightly until established, and harvest the small, sweet blue berries in mid-to-late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, acidic, consistently moist ground.

directions: A hardy, low-growing circumpolar shrub of bogs and tundra that bears small, sweet, blue-black berries. It needs cool conditions, acidic peaty soil, and steady moisture to thrive. Set plants in a damp, sunny-to-lightly-shaded spot, mulch to keep roots cool, and pick the fruit as it ripens in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, acidic mountain-type soil.

directions: A low, mat-forming subalpine huckleberry from the Pacific Northwest mountains, prized for its sweet, flavorful blue berries. It suits cool climates and gritty, humus-rich, acidic soil with good drainage. Plant in dappled sun to full sun where summers are mild, keep evenly moist, and harvest the delicious fruit in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in frost-free, high-elevation tropical conditions.

directions: A small evergreen shrub native to Hawaiian volcanic highlands that bears colorful red-to-yellow berries with a tart-sweet flavor. It favors well-drained volcanic or gritty acidic soil and mild, frost-free conditions. Plant in full sun, water moderately, and harvest the bright berries once they soften and color fully.

planting.note: Plant in spring in wet, acidic, peaty ground.

directions: A creeping, wiry-stemmed cranberry of northern bogs that produces small, tart, speckled red berries. It requires constantly moist, highly acidic peat and cool conditions to establish. Plant the trailing stems into damp ground, keep them saturated, and harvest the firm berries in fall after they redden.

planting.note: Plant in spring in acidic, well-drained soil as a groundcover.

directions: A low, mat-forming evergreen blueberry from the Southeast that works as an ornamental groundcover with edible small blue-black fruit. Its trailing, rooting stems knit together to cover ground in sun or light shade. Plant in acidic, well-drained soil, water until established, and enjoy the modest crop of sweet berries in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, moist, acidic woodland soil.

directions: A deciduous woodland huckleberry of cool coastal and mountain forests that bears early, sweet-tart blue berries. It favors moist, humus-rich, acidic soil and tolerates considerable shade. Plant in dappled light, keep the roots cool and moist with leaf-litter mulch, and pick the fruit as it softens through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, acidic, high-elevation conditions.

directions: A high-altitude South American shrub that produces small, dark purple, antioxidant-rich berries traditionally gathered in the Andes. It prefers cool, humid, acidic soils and mild temperatures without hard freezes. Plant in full sun to light shade, water moderately, and harvest the sweet-tart berries when they fully darken.

planting.note: Set out hardy dormant canes in early spring in most northern gardens.

directions: Plant these sturdy, disease-tolerant canes shallowly and stake or trellis them for support. This old but reliable summer bearer yields medium-large, light red berries on strong stems that tolerate wet ground better than many. Remove spent canes after fruiting and keep the row narrow by cutting stray suckers.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring, spacing generously for the large plants.

directions: Give this vigorous, nearly spineless variety extra room and firm support since the canes grow tall and heavy. It bears some of the largest red raspberries of any cultivar, mild and sweet, ripening in midsummer. Because it suckers little, propagate by tip layering and prune out fruited canes each year.

planting.note: Plant in early spring for a first-year fall crop on the new canes.

directions: Set canes shallowly in full sun and, for the simplest care, mow the entire planting to the ground each winter to grow it as a fall bearer. It produces firm, dark red, aromatic berries on the current season's growth. Keep the row weeded and watered while fruit sizes in late summer.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring for an autumn crop the same year.

directions: Plant this early-ripening fall bearer where it gets full sun and steady moisture. It yields large, firm berries a couple of weeks ahead of most everbearers, extending the harvest window. For a single heavy fall crop, cut all canes to ground level while dormant.

planting.note: Plant in early spring for fall fruit the first season.

directions: Space these strong canes in a sunny, well-drained bed and support them with a simple trellis. This heat-tolerant fall bearer holds large, firm, dark red berries well in warm climates and resists many root diseases. Cut all canes down in late winter to grow it as a single-crop fall raspberry.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring for an early fall harvest.

directions: Plant in full sun and mow the bed down each winter for the easiest fall-bearing management. It ripens early among the everbearers, producing sweet, deep red, conical berries with excellent flavor. Water consistently as the crop develops and pick every few days once fruit begins to color.

subcategory: cane fruit, trailing red raspberry for the South

planting.note: Plant in late winter to early spring in warm southern gardens.

directions: Train the long trailing canes on a trellis or fence in a spot with full sun and good drainage. Bred for heat and humidity, this raspberry is one of the few that fruits reliably in the Deep South, bearing medium red berries best used fresh or in preserves. Tip-layer the cane ends to make new plants and thin old canes after harvest.

planting.note: Plant in late winter or early spring in mild-winter regions.

directions: Give this heat-adapted raspberry full sun in cooler climates but light afternoon shade where summers are scorching. It tolerates warm nights that stop most raspberries and can crop in both summer and fall, producing large, firm red berries. Keep it well watered through hot weather and support the tall canes on wires.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in autumn or early spring in temperate gardens.

directions: Plant this compact, undemanding British variety in full sun and give it a low support of posts and wires. It bears early to mid-season, producing sweet, medium red berries on a plant that suckers modestly and shows good virus tolerance. Prune out old fruited canes at the base each winter.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in autumn or early spring.

directions: Give this vigorous early variety full sun and sturdy wire supports, as the canes grow tall and produce many suckers. It is among the first raspberries to ripen, carrying heavy crops of large, soft red berries good for immediate eating. Thin crowded canes and remove fruited wood after harvest.

subcategory: cane fruit, spine-free floricane red raspberry

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in autumn or early spring.

directions: Plant this smooth, nearly spineless Scottish variety in full sun with a two-wire trellis for support. It crops early with large, bright red, well-flavored berries and is easy to pick thanks to its lack of thorns. Cut fruited canes to the ground after harvest and keep the row well weeded.

subcategory: cane fruit, spine-free floricane red raspberry

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in autumn or early spring.

directions: Set this upright, spine-free variety in full sun and tie the stiff canes to wires. It ripens in mid-season, bearing firm, medium-sized red berries that hold their shape and travel well. Prune out the old fruited canes each winter and mulch to feed the roots.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in autumn or early spring.

directions: Give this heavy-cropping Scottish variety full sun and strong support, since it suckers freely and grows tall. It bears abundant small-to-medium red berries over a long early-to-mid season, ideal for jam and freezing. Keep the spreading row contained by removing wandering suckers and cut out fruited canes annually.

subcategory: cane fruit, spine-free floricane red raspberry

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in autumn or early spring.

directions: Plant this smooth-caned early variety in full sun with wires to hold the crop upright. It produces firm, bright red, well-sized berries early in the season on thornless stems that are pleasant to pick. Remove fruited canes after harvest and feed with compost each spring.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: Give this tall, vigorous summer bearer full sun and firm trellising to carry the crop. It ripens late for a summer type, producing large, conical, richly flavored red berries. Cut out the fruited floricanes each year and thin the new canes to a manageable row.

subcategory: cane fruit, early floricane red raspberry

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: Plant this hardy, early-ripening variety in full sun with a simple trellis. It is one of the first summer raspberries to color up, bearing medium-large, firm red berries on productive canes. Remove old fruited wood after picking and keep the planting mulched and evenly watered.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: Plant this Pacific Northwest variety in full sun and rich, well-drained soil, as it tolerates heavier ground and root rot better than most. It bears medium-large, dark red, aromatic berries in midsummer. Trellis the canes, remove fruited wood in fall, and keep the crown from staying waterlogged.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: Set this vigorous British Columbia variety in full sun with strong trellis wires. It yields large, firm, glossy red berries on tall canes in mid-season and performs well in cooler coastal climates. Prune out fruited floricanes each year and thin the new canes for airflow.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: Plant this hardy, productive variety in full sun and support the canes on wires. It bears large, firm, medium-red berries over a long mid-season and tolerates cold winters well. Remove old fruited canes after harvest and keep the row narrow by cutting stray suckers.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant black raspberries away from any red raspberries to limit virus spread and set the crowns just below the soil line. This variety ripens its large, glossy black berries all at once for an easy concentrated harvest. Tip the arching canes in summer to force branching, and remove fruited canes after picking.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: Site this vigorous black raspberry in full sun and keep it well separated from red raspberries. It produces heavy crops of medium-large black berries on spreading canes that root where the tips touch soil. Pinch growing tips at about waist height to build fruiting laterals and cut out old canes each year.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: Plant this productive black raspberry in full sun and well-drained soil, away from red raspberry plantings. It bears large, firm black berries in a concentrated mid-summer season and shows good vigor. Tip the primocanes in summer to encourage laterals and prune fruited canes to the ground after harvest.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring.

directions: Plant this cold-hardy purple raspberry in full sun and manage it much like a black raspberry, keeping it apart from reds. It bears dusky purple berries with a distinctive tangy, full flavor prized for pies and preserves. Tip the canes in summer to promote branching and remove fruited wood after the crop.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in late winter to early spring.

directions: Plant this erect Arkansas blackberry in full sun and stiff soil, spacing plants a few feet apart in the row. It produces very large, firm, glossy black berries with high yields on strong, self-supporting but thorny canes. Cut out fruited floricanes after harvest and shorten primocanes in summer to build a stocky, branching hedge.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in late winter to early spring.

directions: Set this early-ripening erect blackberry in full sun with good air movement. It bears medium, sweet, small-seeded black berries ahead of most other varieties, on thorny upright canes. Tip the primocanes at about four feet to force lateral branches and remove fruited canes at the end of the season.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in late winter to early spring.

directions: Plant this early erect blackberry in full sun in a warm-climate garden. It produces large, sweet black berries on vigorous thorny canes and was one of the first improved Arkansas selections. Cut out the fruited canes after harvest and tip-prune the new canes in summer to keep them stocky.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring in colder regions.

directions: Choose this variety where winters are too cold for typical southern blackberries, as it survives well below zero. Plant in full sun and let the semi-erect thorny canes fill a trellised row, producing medium, sweet-tart black berries in mid to late summer. Prune out fruited canes each year and mulch the roots for winter protection.

planting.note: Plant in early spring for a first-year fall crop on new canes.

directions: Plant this thorny primocane blackberry in full sun where it can fruit on both old and new canes. For a fall crop of medium black berries, let the current-season canes flower and set fruit, then cut them back after harvest. Tipping the primocanes in early summer increases branching and the number of berries.

planting.note: Plant in early spring for a first-year fall crop on new canes.

directions: Set this thorny primocane variety in full sun and treat it for a late-summer to fall crop on the year's new growth. It bears medium black berries and, like its sibling Prime-Jan, benefits from soft-tipping the canes to boost fruiting laterals. In warm climates it can produce two crops a year with careful pruning.

planting.note: Plant dormant plants in early spring and train onto a trellis.

directions: Train the long, thornless trailing canes of this variety along a two-wire trellis in full sun. It produces large, glossy, sweet black berries with excellent flavor over a long early summer season. Tie the new primocanes up along the wire as they grow and remove the fruited canes after harvest.

planting.note: Plant dormant plants in early spring and train onto a trellis.

directions: Give this vigorous thornless trailing variety a strong trellis and plenty of room, as its canes grow very long. It bears exceptionally large, firm, sweet black berries in early summer and is easy to harvest without thorns. Train the new canes on wires and cut the fruited ones out at the base after picking.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in late winter to early spring.

directions: Plant this thornless erect variety in full sun in the Southeast, where it ripens later than most and extends the harvest. It bears firm, sweet black berries on productive canes and holds up well to summer heat. Tip the primocanes in summer for branching and remove fruited canes after the crop.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in late winter to early spring.

directions: Plant this newer thornless Arkansas variety in full sun for some of the sweetest berries among erect blackberries. It bears medium-large, very sweet black fruit on compact, self-supporting canes. Cut out fruited floricanes each year and tip the new canes in summer to encourage sturdy branching.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in late winter to early spring.

directions: Set this thornless erect variety in full sun where it produces large, firm, uniform black berries in mid-season. The plants are vigorous and self-supporting, making them easy to manage in a home row. Remove fruited canes after harvest and tip the primocanes in summer to build a branching hedge.

planting.note: Plant dormant plants in early spring and train to a trellis.

directions: Train this thornless trailing variety along a sturdy two-wire trellis in full sun. It produces glossy, firm, medium-large black berries with a mild, sweet flavor in early to mid-summer. Guide the new primocanes onto the wire and cut the fruited canes out at ground level after harvest.

planting.note: Plant dormant plants in early spring and train to a trellis.

directions: Train the trailing canes of this early variety along a trellis in full sun for the season's first blackberries. It bears large, sweet, glossy black berries well ahead of most other types. Tie in the new primocanes as they grow and remove the fruited canes after picking.

planting.note: Plant dormant plants in early spring and train to a trellis.

directions: Plant this compact thornless trailing variety in full sun on a trellis, where its shorter canes make it easy to contain. It bears large, firm, glossy black berries with good flavor in mid-summer. Train the new canes along the wire and cut out the fruited ones at the base after harvest.

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in early spring in colder gardens.

directions: Choose this old, very cold-hardy erect variety for northern gardens where tender blackberries fail. Plant in full sun and let the upright canes carry their early crop of medium, sweet-tart black berries. Prune out fruited canes each year and mulch the crowns for winter.

subcategory: cane fruit, dwarf thornless container blackberry

planting.note: Plant in spring in a large pot or bed after hard frosts pass.

directions: This compact, thornless dwarf variety is bred for patio pots and small gardens, staying only a few feet tall. Grow it in full sun in a roomy container with rich, well-drained mix, and it can fruit on both old and new canes for a summer and fall crop. Water regularly, and in cold zones shelter the pot over winter.

subcategory: cane fruit, thorny erect blackberry for the South

planting.note: Plant dormant canes in late winter to early spring in warm regions.

directions: Plant this Southern-adapted erect blackberry in full sun where it tolerates heat and humidity well. It bears sweet, medium-large black berries late in the season on thorny, upright canes. Cut out fruited canes after harvest and tip the new canes in summer to build a branching, productive hedge.

planting.note: Plant dormant plants in early spring and train to a trellis.

directions: This trailing blackberry-dewberry-raspberry hybrid needs a warm site, full sun, and a strong trellis for its long canes. It produces large, dark reddish-purple to black berries that are sweet and juicy, excellent fresh or in preserves. Train the new canes on wires and cut fruited canes out after harvest.

planting.note: Plant dormant plants in early spring and train to a trellis.

directions: This spine-free form of the tayberry has long trailing canes that are easy to handle on a trellis in full sun. It bears large, long, dark red raspberry-blackberry hybrid berries with an aromatic, tangy flavor in early summer. Train the new canes along the wire and cut out fruited canes after harvest.

planting.note: Plant dormant plants in early spring and train to a trellis.

directions: Give this vigorous New Zealand hybrid a strong trellis and full sun for its very long trailing canes. It produces exceptionally large, long, glossy black berries over an unusually extended season. Train the new canes carefully because of the thorns, and remove fruited canes at the base after harvest.

planting.note: Set dormant bare-root or potted bushes in late winter or early spring while the soil is still cool.

directions: Plant bushes two inches deeper than they grew in the nursery to encourage strong new shoots from the base. This vigorous Scottish selection resists mildew, gall mite, and leaf spot, making it a reliable garden currant. Pick the clusters when every berry has turned matte black and pulls free easily, usually in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant while dormant in early spring or during a mild autumn spell.

directions: Dig in plenty of compost before planting and firm the roots well against settling. This compact early cropper carries resistance to reversion virus and its aphid carrier, so keep it free of stray suckers. Harvest the short strigs in early to midsummer once the fruit colours fully and sweetens.

planting.note: Best planted bare-root during the dormant season into deep, moisture-retentive ground.

directions: Space bushes generously and mulch each spring to hold moisture through fruiting. As the latest-ripening of the Ben series, it flowers after most frost danger has passed, which suits colder gardens. Gather the large glossy berries in late summer when the whole strig has darkened.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring, spacing bushes for good air movement.

directions: Work in rotted manure at planting and cut all shoots back hard the first winter to build a bushy framework. Prized for unusually large, sweet berries that are pleasant fresh off the bush. Harvest the early crop in midsummer as soon as the fruit softens and dulls in colour.

planting.note: Set out dormant bushes in early spring in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: Plant deeply into rich soil and remove weak stems to leave an open, spreading bush. This old mid-season variety is heavy-cropping with sweet, well-flavoured fruit though its early flowers can be caught by late frost. Pick the trailing strigs from mid to late summer when fully coloured.

planting.note: A cold-hardy Swedish sort best planted dormant in early spring.

directions: Choose a site with reliable moisture and plant firmly, then mulch to keep roots cool. Extremely winter-hardy and productive, it flowers late enough to dodge most spring frosts in northern gardens. Harvest the uniform berries in mid to late summer as each strig turns fully black.

planting.note: Plant dormant bushes in early spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Enrich the planting hole and settle the roots deep to promote strong basal growth. This classic French variety is the traditional base for cassis liqueur, with small but intensely aromatic berries. Pick in midsummer when the fruit is fully black, accepting a modest yield in exchange for outstanding flavour.

planting.note: Plant while dormant into deep soil enriched with organic matter.

directions: Firm the roots well and prune out a third of the oldest wood each winter to keep the bush productive. This old late-flowering variety, also known as Black Naples, bears large sweet berries and often escapes frost damage. Harvest the loose strigs in mid to late summer once fully ripe.

planting.note: Plant dormant on a short leg (single stem) in early spring for an open goblet bush.

directions: Unlike black currants, plant red currants at their original soil level and rub off low buds to form a clear stem. This heavy early cropper produces long strigs of bright, tart berries on old and new wood, so prune to build a permanent framework. Harvest whole trusses in early summer when the berries are fully red and translucent.

planting.note: Plant dormant bushes in early spring, keeping the stem clear of soil.

directions: Give this French variety a sunny sheltered wall or open bed and shorten side shoots in summer to boost fruiting spurs. It is early, vigorous, and very productive, holding its firm berries well on the bush. Cut the long strigs in early summer when uniformly coloured for jelly, juice, or fresh use.

planting.note: Set out dormant plants in early spring in fertile, free-draining soil.

directions: Plant at the nursery soil line and train to an open-centred bush for easy picking. This Dutch mid-season variety flowers late, resists frost, and crops reliably even in cooler summers. Harvest the deep red, slightly tart berries in mid to late summer by snipping whole strigs.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring, spacing for airflow and light.

directions: Keep a clear leg and prune to a framework of eight to ten main branches for a productive goblet. This late-season variety carries long strigs of large, sweet-sharp berries that hang well past ripening. Pick trusses in late summer once fully coloured, or leave them to sweeten further on the bush.

planting.note: Plant dormant bushes in early spring in a sunny, sheltered position.

directions: Set at the original planting depth and shorten laterals through summer to encourage spur growth. This vigorous late Dutch variety produces heavy yields of firm, tart berries that store well after picking. Gather the medium strigs in late summer when uniformly red.

planting.note: Plant dormant stock in early spring into well-worked ground.

directions: Plant on a short leg and remove crossing shoots to keep the centre open. This old American variety is an early, prolific cropper bearing large bright red berries on long stalks. Harvest whole trusses in early to midsummer when the fruit turns a clear translucent red.

planting.note: Plant dormant bushes in early spring at their original soil level.

directions: Keep a clear stem and prune to an open goblet so light reaches the fruiting spurs. This heritage white currant bears large, sweet, low-acid berries that are pleasant eaten straight from the bush. Pick the pale translucent strigs in midsummer once the fruit yellows and softens slightly.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Set at soil level and thin the oldest branches each winter to maintain vigour. This productive Slovakian variety carries long strigs of sweet, pale creamy-yellow berries. Harvest in midsummer when the whole truss is fully coloured and the fruit is soft.

planting.note: Plant dormant bushes on a short leg in early spring for easy picking among the thorns.

directions: Plant at the nursery soil line and prune to an open goblet so you can reach the fruit between the spines. This old English dessert variety bears pale yellow-green, sweet, aromatic berries in heavy crops. Thin some fruit in early summer for cooking, then let the rest ripen to full size and softness for eating fresh.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring in a sunny spot with good drainage.

directions: Give this compact, upright bush a clear leg and open centre to improve air flow and reduce mildew. It produces small, richly flavoured golden-yellow dessert berries on a tidy plant well suited to small gardens. Pick in midsummer when the fruit is soft, amber, and fully sweet.

planting.note: Plant dormant bushes in early spring into fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Prune to an open framework and mulch to keep the roots evenly moist through the fruiting season. This English variety produces small, thin-skinned, almost transparent white berries with exceptional sweet flavour. Harvest in midsummer when the fruit is soft and translucent, handling gently as the skins are delicate.

planting.note: Plant dormant on a short leg in early spring for a manageable thorny bush.

directions: Set at soil level and thin the branches to let light and air into the centre, which helps limit mildew. This old Lancashire show variety bears large, hairy, dark red berries good for cooking early or dessert when fully ripe. Pick the culinary crop in early summer and leave the rest to sweeten into midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant bushes in early spring in a sunny, sheltered bed.

directions: Give this vigorous, spreading bush plenty of room and prune to keep the fruiting wood accessible. A famous Victorian exhibition variety, it produces very large red-green berries prized for their size. Thin heavily in early summer for the biggest fruit, then harvest the remainder ripe in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant on a clear leg in early spring in fertile soil.

directions: Prune to an open goblet and mulch each spring to hold moisture around the roots. This old English variety carries large, hairy, dark red berries that cook well and sweeten nicely when ripe. Pick early for tart culinary fruit or leave until midsummer for full dessert flavour.

planting.note: Plant dormant bushes in early spring in a warm, sunny position.

directions: Set at soil level and keep the centre open so the early crop ripens evenly. As one of the first gooseberries to mature, it bears small, hairy, golden-yellow berries with a sweet, honeyed flavour. Harvest from early summer once the fruit turns bright yellow and gives slightly to the touch.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; its early flowers appreciate a sheltered spot.

directions: Plant on a short leg and prune to an open bush, protecting the early blossom from hard frost. This reliable variety bears large, pale green, slightly hairy berries suited to both cooking and dessert. Thin for cooking in early summer and let the rest ripen to sweetness by midsummer.

planting.note: Plant dormant bushes in early spring in fertile, well-drained ground.

directions: Prune to keep the centre open and mulch to conserve moisture through fruiting. This dependable old variety is a heavy, mildew-tolerant cropper bearing large pale green berries with good flavour. Harvest in mid to late summer when the fruit softens and turns yellowish-green.

planting.note: A hardy Finnish selection, best planted dormant in early spring.

directions: Plant on a short leg and open the centre to improve air movement and mildew resistance. Bred in Finland for cold tolerance and disease resistance, it bears greenish-yellow berries with a sweet, tangy flavour. Harvest in midsummer when the fruit is full-sized and slightly soft.

planting.note: Plant dormant bushes in early spring in a sunny, open site.

directions: Prune to an open goblet to aid its natural resistance to mildew and leaf spot. This modern variety produces medium green berries with a fresh, sharp flavour ideal for cooking. Pick the early crop in midsummer while still firm and green.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring in fertile, free-draining soil.

directions: Give this upright bush an open framework and mulch to keep moisture steady. A German-bred variety with good mildew resistance, it produces dark red, sweet dessert berries. Harvest in early to midsummer when the fruit deepens to red and softens.

planting.note: Plant dormant in early spring; the near-thornless stems make siting easy.

directions: Prune lightly to keep the centre open, taking advantage of the almost spineless growth for easy picking. This Swiss variety bears large reddish dessert berries early in the season and resists mildew well. Harvest in early summer once the fruit turns pink-red and softens.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; seed needs a cold, moist stratification to break dormancy.

directions: Set plants at soil level and shear as needed, since this tough shrub tolerates hard pruning and makes a dense hedge. Grown mainly as ornamental foliage, its small greenish flowers give way to sparse red berries on female plants. Expect the neat, mildew-prone foliage to leaf out early; clip after flowering to keep a tidy form.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty, well-drained soil; seed benefits from cold stratification.

directions: Give this drought-tolerant western native lean, sharply drained soil and full sun. Its small tubular white-to-pink flowers attract hummingbirds and are followed by bright red berries. Water sparingly once established, as it is adapted to dry mountain slopes and dislikes soggy ground.

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained soil; seed needs cold-moist stratification.

directions: Site this Californian native in partial shade with fast drainage and handle it with care, as the stems and fruit are very spiny. Showy red-and-white fuchsia-like flowers give way to purple berries covered in stiff bristles. Give little summer water once established, matching its dry-slope habitat.

planting.note: Plant in fall so roots establish through the mild, wet season of its native climate.

directions: Plant this California native in well-drained soil and expect it to bloom in fall and winter when little else flowers. Pink to purple flower clusters draw hummingbirds and are followed by dusty blue-black berries. Keep it dry through summer, when the shrub is naturally drought-deciduous and rests.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; seed requires cold-moist stratification to germinate.

directions: Plant this eastern North American native in a woodland edge with moist, humus-rich soil. Its greenish-yellow bell flowers become reddish-purple berries covered in long spines, giving it the name dogberry. The fruit is edible when ripe despite the prickly husk, and the shrub tolerates light shade well.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall; stratify seed cold and moist before sowing.

directions: Give this hardy midwestern native ordinary garden soil and part shade to full sun. Small white-green flowers on long stalks give way to smooth purple-black berries that ripen in summer. The spiny stems form a thicket, so site it where its arching thorny growth won't crowd paths.

planting.note: Plant in spring in consistently moist, shaded ground; seed needs cold stratification.

directions: Plant this Pacific Northwest native in damp, rich soil along stream banks or in shaded borders. The large maple-like leaves are pungent when crushed, and long upright racemes of greenish flowers yield blue-black berries with a whitish bloom. Keep the soil reliably moist, as it grows naturally in wet coastal woodlands.
